title: Strings — Regular Expressions and Encoding
key_insight: Real-world text is messy. Regular expressions let you find patterns in text — not just exact matches. Encoding awareness prevents silent data corruption when handling non-ASCII text.
body:
## Regular Expressions — Pattern Matching

```python
import re

text = 'Contact us at support@example.com or sales@company.org'

# Find all email addresses
emails = re.findall(r'[\w.]+@[\w.]+\.[a-z]{2,}', text)
print(emails)  # ['support@example.com', 'sales@company.org']

# Common patterns:
# \d — any digit (0-9)
# \w — word character (letter, digit, underscore)
# \s — whitespace
# .  — any character except newline
# *  — 0 or more of the previous
# +  — 1 or more of the previous
# ?  — 0 or 1 of the previous
# [] — character class: [a-z] = any lowercase letter
# ^  — start of string
# $  — end of string
# {n,m} — between n and m repetitions

# Validate a UK postcode:
pattern = r'^[A-Z]{1,2}\d[A-Z\d]?\s?\d[A-Z]{2}$'
print(bool(re.match(pattern, 'SW1A 2AA')))  # True
print(bool(re.match(pattern, 'not-valid'))) # False
```

## Groups and Named Groups

```python
# Groups let you extract parts of a match
date_pattern = r'(\d{4})-(\d{2})-(\d{2})'
text = 'Published on 2024-03-15'

match = re.search(date_pattern, text)
if match:
    year, month, day = match.groups()
    print(f'Year: {year}, Month: {month}, Day: {day}')

# Named groups are clearer:
pattern = r'(?P<year>\d{4})-(?P<month>\d{2})-(?P<day>\d{2})'
match = re.search(pattern, text)
if match:
    print(match.group('year'))  # '2024'
```

## String Encoding: Bytes vs Strings

```python
# In Python 3, str is Unicode, bytes is raw bytes

text = 'Hello, 世界'  # Unicode string (Python str)
bytes_utf8 = text.encode('utf-8')   # encode str → bytes
bytes_utf8 = text.encode('utf-8')   # 13 bytes (Chinese chars take 3 bytes each)
print(bytes_utf8)  # b'Hello, \xe4\xb8\x96\xe7\x95\x8c'

back = bytes_utf8.decode('utf-8')  # decode bytes → str
print(back == text)  # True

# Common encoding issues:
try:
    with open('file.txt', 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:  # always specify encoding
        content = f.read()
except UnicodeDecodeError:
    with open('file.txt', 'r', encoding='latin-1') as f:  # try latin-1 as fallback
        content = f.read()
```

## Advanced f-String Features

```python
from datetime import datetime

name  = 'Alice'
gpa   = 3.87654
today = datetime.now()

print(f'{name!r}')              # 'Alice' (repr)
print(f'{gpa:.2f}')             # 3.88
print(f'{1_000_000:,}')         # 1,000,000
print(f'{today:%Y-%m-%d}')      # 2024-03-15
print(f'{name:>20}')            # right-align in 20 chars
print(f'{"TITLE":^20}')         # centre in 20 chars
print(f'{gpa = }')              # gpa = 3.87654 (debug format, Python 3.8+)
```
common_mistakes: Using re.match() vs re.search(): match() only checks the START of the string; search() scans the whole string
Greedy vs non-greedy: `.*` is greedy (matches as much as possible); `.*?` is non-greedy. `<.*>` on `text` matches everything including tags; `<.*?>` matches only ``
Forgetting to encode files correctly — always specify encoding='utf-8' when opening text files
mini_exercise: Write a regex to extract all phone numbers from a string. Phone numbers look like: `+44 7911 123456`, `07911 123456`, or `0207-123-4567`. Test it on a string containing 3 different formats.
real_world_usage: Log parsing in production systems uses regular expressions constantly: extracting IP addresses, timestamps, and error codes from millions of log lines per second. Cloudflare's WAF (Web Application Firewall) uses regex patterns to detect malicious requests. Google's search indexer parses structured data with regex.
explain_back_prompt: What is the difference between re.match() and re.search()? What is greedy matching and when would you use non-greedy? What is the difference between a Python `str` and `bytes`?
